comments.title: Halloween Town
comments.content:
This is a question for JMG and any other readers who've noticed stuff like this. On Saturday I took a walk through my intensely liberal, PMC American neighborhood and saw the following:

4-5 elaborate cemetery lawn installations with fancy plastic tombstones, a dozen other lawns with simpler tombstone decorations

Two zombie yard installations including life-sized standing zombie figures and zombies emerging from the ground

Around four giant spiderweb installations with spiderwebs made out of rope nets about 5 feet at the widest point, inhabited by giant spiders made of pipe cleaners about 2 feet wide

One rope spiderweb covering an entire side of a house with two big model spiders about 4 feet wide

At least four houses whose fences or porches were completely encased in wispy artificial spiderweb

Two or three giant ghost installations, skeletal figures wreathed in robes over 7 feet tall

Two grim reaper installations featuring a human-sized skeleton with a cloak and scythe, one had electronic glowing eyes

About three giant skeleton installations, plastic model skeletons over 10 feet tall standing in someone's yard

At least 20 pairs of skeletal hands or feet sticking up from the ground

Two inflatable installations, one of a giant octopus and one of the Bluey cartoon characters in mummy wrapping, requiring attached air compressors to keep them inflated

Two ghost ship installations, simulated ship prows emerging from a lawn with a pirate skeleton standing aboard

Dozens of trees bedecked with ornaments, four or five trees containing skeletons or mannequins

Four or five houses with over 3 skeletons visible in the yard, including elaborate setups like a skeleton sitting on the roof posed like a cowboy riding a bull

Hundreds of simple jack-o-lantern, ghost, bat and witch decorations

And lastly:

A school flanked by the same giant 10 foot+ skeletons I saw in the yards, along with balconies adorned with life-sized figures of at least half a dozen movie slashers, including Jason Voorhees, Freddy Krueger, Jigsaw and Michael Myers, a giant skeleton appearing to emerge from the ground, dummies hanging from trees and most every other imaginable spooky decoration


It's safe to say Halloween draws far more decorative devotion than any other holiday around here. The closest competitor may have been the last election; I saw many items promoting Kamala Harris including many homemade ones. There was genuine organic enthusiasm for her here. For Christmas there are a few big yard displays and many houses with lights set up, but the enthusiasm and creativity is nowhere near that of Halloween. One of the giant skeleton houses keeps theirs on the front lawn year-round, along with a set of doll heads painted white with the eyes blacked out hanging from the tree in front like Christmas ornaments. It's an expensive house too, and one that I've always felt an off vibe from.

What do you make of this? Does anyone else live in places that resemble annexes of Hell at this time of year? I feel like it's exposing some darkness in the subconscious of the people who live here. I enjoy spooky stuff as much as anyone and I grew up in a neighborhood where lots of people decorated for Halloween, but this feels different, there's something sinister about the magnitude and focus of this activity.

In my very working class suburb of Toronto, there is one house on a corner lot of my street that already has a gigantic skeleton, Shelob-sized spider (with web), tombstones, and skeletal hands emerging from the front lawn. The owners of the property are Chinese.

Some distance away there are two houses side-by-side that cover their entire lawns with Hallowe'en stuff. Most of it is already up. It is practically a tourist destination. One household is ethnically English; I'm not sure about the other but there are very few Mexicans in these parts of town. The good thing (if such a thing can be termed 'good') is the same houses do it all over again for Christmas (different decorations, of course). It is quite the sight to behold!

Until about 10 years ago, the only "decorations" that one could find on Hallowe'en was the age-old Jack O'Lantern. It seems to me that the Hallowe'en decoration fad seems to have ramped up significantly post-Covid. I wonder if there is something going on subconsciously in our society in terms of processing the morbid psy-ops that we were subjected to for several years straight... Even if there is a 'push' to market this stuff, there has to be a 'pull' on the public's side to actually buy it.

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Halloween Town
replies.content: I was musing the other day about the continuing cultural fascination with zombies etc, and it came to my mind that perhaps it’s a subconscious acknowledgement of our current state of society on a physical level even, to say nothing of the cultural and social decay we’re all suffering under. The train of thought started when I heard secondhand news that a woman I went to school with was back in hospital, again. This person was born with significant physical malformations and by the time I knew her in late elementary school she had already had close to 30 surgeries to patch her back together, she regularly still was fed through a tube, and so on. Her entire life has been in and out of hospitals and entirely dependent on medical interventions and operations. She is, more or less, one of the living dead, I guess? If she had been born at any other time in history she wouldn’t have survived more than a few hours after birth. I mean, all of us of course are here for only a short while and could die at any moment. But think of all the people you know who are literally being kept alive by the medical industry, either through procedures or drugs. I don’t mean to judge anyone on the state of their soul in all this, just noting that on a physical level at least, we are surrounded by… not-quite-allowed-to-die bodies… medical industry zombies?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
When you say it that way, it connotatively centers a framing under which Protestants "already knew" that there was a relevant moral binary under which their options were blind belief in dogma vs. an intelligent relationship with the universe etc. Avoiding this connotation would have looked like something in the direction of, "Blind terrified fear of being deceived (even if by an admittedly very confusing and actively-inscrutable and notably somewhat inherently-illusion-fostering universe of semi-subjectivities) if one departs from dogma, unfortunately, nonetheless actually fails to substitute for an intelligent relationship" etc.

That still didn't specify the nuance about "and for all they know the deception will chain into being tempted into being willfully irreversibly deluded, so this initial step could already be their final chance for all eternity", or "they have already been taught that the only relevant moralizable binary is instead whether or not they are relying on pride of the intellect; according to that binary, full submission to the divine will and full compliance with its infallibly revealed standard of doxastic orthopraxy is the only thing that constitutes proper full consent to be protected and vaguely-potentially-adequate nonconsent to be deceived, whereas meanwhile the deceivers in question have an archangelic amount of power and cunning to invent and enact temptations and deceptions", or "they have already been taught that the relevant moralizable binary additionally has their own wicked licentiousness-rationalization working for its opposite side, prowling around like a desperately ravenous rat trying every angle of entry". For some reason, our language and set of shared concepts makes these nuances very hard to express in sufficiently few words to not be burdensome.

But the existence of those strong contrary nuances is why you would want to avoid presenting a clearly very-moralizable binary in a way that so readily lent itself to being interpreted as agreement with what the implied moralism would be, even if the literal reading happened to correspond to the point you were trying to get at. (Part of what it means for a given phrasing to readily lend itself to being interpreted a certain way is that there was some alternative phrasing not so many words away, and thus not costing that much more to communicate, that would have clearly disclaimed the obvious connotation -- in this case, either by disclaiming it directly, or by supplying, as I did above, a sketch of the necessary alternate psychological explanation for how Protestants could act this way without being knowingly morally culpable. Another part of what it means is for the phrasing to be presented in a venue where expressions of moral sentiment are flying around all the time, and/or by a party known to express moral sentiments all the time, with at best only rare marking of how some ambiguous statements were meant to be interpreted merely literally without the implied moralism, since that makes it much more plausible that an ambiguous statement was meant to be interpreted as another expression of moral sentiment in with the others. Both of these effects feed into the always-present interpretational game-theoretic-equilibrium feedback cycle of "I suppose, that you could have supposed, that I could have supposed, that you could have supposed, [...] that I could have supposed, that you meant to convey X, from how you didn't take on the additional effort and/or risks of doing something that would have more conveyed Y instead". I know it sounds complicated when I write it out like that, but people's social brains are subconsciously doing calculations like that all the time, and memorizing the more commonly-appearing interpretation steps in large blocks, like how chess grandmasters build up internal symbols that encode recognizable strategically-relevant configurations of three or four pieces together.)

If you don't know that germs exist, and try to deal with them by blind belief in religious doctrines, you can end up stone cold dead; that's a statement of simple fact, not a complicated value judgment involving moral culpability and the rest of the overelaborate mechanism you've deployed here. comments.title: Strangeness of universe
comments.content:
It's taken me a good long time, including a period of "what if eternity is broken" pointless worry (which seems silly now, but it wasn't at the time) but I have sort of reached the conclusion that the universe being blithely unaware of humans (and presumably, multitudes of other life forms too) is actually a good thing. If the universe/cosmos was set up for humans, with humans having a prominent role in it, it would be the Christian heaven/hell dichotomy. It would also be the abuser God, because whatever God which put such a structure in place was most likely a bully, and that in turn doesn't seem particularly god-like behaviour.

So a strange, indifferent, universe, basically blind Azatoth is arguably better than all that in that it provides for freedom without the overall judging eye of the gods, for the most part.

But there's still a catch that bothers me. You have said before that the gods aren't ethical, or more precisely, they reflect a few singular values, and human beings can also come to worship entities that aren't exactly gods. The Aztec deities always trouble me in this regard.

I do wonder if that's just a downstream feature of the strangeness, I would expect that if an egregore around an ethic, or a moral, built up over time and countless civilisations it could create power enough to attract the interest of a god, who at least would understand the basis of karma and potentially lend some weight to it. However,I guess I am still troubled by the concept that some gods might have ethics that are entirely opposed to what we tend to think are accepted civilised human ethics.

replies.title: Re: Strangeness of universe
replies.content:
Keep in mind that morality varies from culture to culture. There were quite a few societies back in the day in which sharing your wife with a guest was an ordinary part of hospitality, and of course there have been plenty of societies in which manslaughter was considered a virtuous act. Each culture is inspired by deities who are in harmony with its ethical tone, and each culture then reaps the collective karma that is brought about by that ethical tone.

The Aztec empire was toward the extreme end -- comparable, say, to the Soviet Union. Its ideology was aberrant, and it suffered the karmic consequences in an unusually complete and hideous way. Other Mesoamerican societies had less bloodthirsty relationships with the same gods and had different destinies.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG, are you familiar with the meditative states (Samadhi) of Buddhism? Particularly the jhanas?

Is there some equivalent method in the West to reach them? So far the sources I've encountered still speak about the Eastern method of reaching it which isn't inherently bad per se but those have never worked for me.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The jhanas, like other states of altered consciousness, aren't reached so much as constructed. If you go over the rupa jhanas, for example, you'll find that each of them involves teaching the mind to follow some aspect of basic Buddhist teaching. Western paths, having their own teachings, won't embody the same ideals, though the principle is there -- for example, when Eliphas Lévi talks about mastering the four elements within the self, he's discussing the same general process of teaching the mind to embody certain ideals.

In general, though, Western meditative paths aren't as fixated on specific exotic states of consciousness as Eastern paths are. It's the broader process, not the specific spasms of awareness, that are important in our Western traditions.

comments.title: Origins of Enneagram
comments.content:
Last week someone inquired about the origins of Gurdjieff's enneagram.

There is an extensive discussion in James Webb's book The Harmonious Circle, mainly pp 505-525, but the digital copy on the Internet Archive is searchable and will highlight all occurrences of the word for the interested reader. Webb first describes a figure published by Athanasius Kircher and speculates why Gurdjieff's version differs slightly from it; then he brings in Francesco Giorgi and the Cabala, and finally, he mentions in passing that the enneagram appears in the Golden Dawn lecture on polygons. This treatment, like the book as a whole, is an ambitious and provocative synthesis of a vast body of data. I'm not sure Webb got everything right but I recommend the book highly.

Gray Hat

replies.content: Thank you for this. The enneagram in the GD knowledge lecture isn't Gurdjieff's enneagram, for whatever that's worth.

replies.title: Re: Listening to Gods
replies.content:
One thing I bear in mind is that while myths convey profound truths, they're still works of fiction. Even when they refer to things that actually happened, like wars, they're still fictionalized accounts. So the question for me is: what are these stories trying to convey about the gods in question?

I'll use Odin as an example, since it's the one I know best: he commits nearly every crime in the book (save defiling sacred land, which is why Fenrir can't be killed). But he is also the wisest of all beings — having paid steep prices to attain that status — and often there's a clear reason for his transgressions. comments.title: Tattoos
comments.content:
The other day I was helping my sister in law pack moving boxes, and we got into a discussion on the topic of tattoos. Apparently she'd seen some exorcist talking about how tattoos are condemned by the Bible and have always been condemned (ignoring the existence of pilgrimage tattoos, I guess), and that an infernally motivated tattoo artist could "curse" the tattoo ink and give you a demonic infestation.

I gave the obvious rebuttal that pilgrimage tattoos have been a thing for literally a thousand years, that demonic possession doesn't really work that way, and that, even if you were afraid of evil satanic tattoo artists, a quick look-see through the artists' social media would probably clue you in as to whether you were getting a tattoo from a bad actor like that. It's not like unethical practitioners have been *quiet* about it lately.

That led me to wonder though if tattoos might suffer from the "yoga problem," that is that it's a deeply ritualistic practice that has been stripped of its associated ascetic preparation, and as such can leave people spiritually vulnerable if done wrong. Most cultures up until now treated tattoos as something you earned, either as a coming-of-age ceremony or from doing something physically and spiritually difficult (making it to Jerusalem on pilgrimage, sailing to Asia from Europe, etc.) All of that is totally absent from modern culture. Even a religious tattoo, when gotten flippantly or without appropriate prayer and fasting beforehand could theoretically be spiritually dubious, without need for satanic panic explanations of curses in the ink.

Am I on the right track with this, or am I off in the weeds?

replies.title: Re: Tattoos

On the "'an it harm none"--some prominent Wiccans took the advice so far that it effectively precluded doing any magic at all. If I do magic to get a job, someone else didn't get the job, therefore I have harmed them. Oh noes! Nice healing spells were about the only thing left or vague wishes for world peace. As for the masochism--well Robert Graves, who shaped the concept of the Triple Goddess in maiden, mother, crone form definitely had a thing about the young woman as inspiration for the poet but eventually rejecting him, etc. As for Gardner--Aiden Kelly in _Crafting the Art of Magick_ reinforced the idea that Gardner was aroused by the flogging in certain rituals. replies.title: Re: Egregor or deity?
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender here)

It is customary, in most varieties of British Wica and its more direct offshoots, to refrain from writing down the proper names or specific titles by which certain deities are spoken of in a cast circle where only initiates are present. Those names or titles are orally disclosed to people after they have been initiated into the tradition. In more public settings the generic "Lord and Lady" is used.

Doreen Valiente, who was one of Gerald Gardner's High Priestesses, produced a liturgical piece called The Prose Charge of the Goddess, or more commonly just The Charge of the Goddess, which has been widely published with many variations. If you see a version which begins "Listen to the words of the Great Mother," or something similar, and has a second section which begins "Hear ye the words of the Star Goddess," or something similar, you are reading a theological statement which is fairly (or as JMG would say, "tolerably") representative of the Wiccan understanding of the nature of the Lady.

This piece dates from some time in the 1950s and contains quotations from Aradia and a few other previous works, but the piece as a whole is credited to Valiente.

comments.title: Hypocrisy and Christian divination

His method involved an invoking prayer and request for guidance then opening the Bible to a random page and reading the first paragraph of text your eyes land on. This passage was then to be interpreted to understand God’s meaning and get your answer. Simply put this is in practice identical to casting cards or a chart.
I started looking into this practice and I have been flummoxed because it just keeps showing up in every denomination since compiling the Bible in one book became a common practice. Further it’s been practiced widely and openly by the most anti-magical Christian sects since the moment of their founding.
Now I think Bibliomancy (to give it a name) should actually work quite well. And the Bible is uniquely well suited to being a divinitory book. Both due to its divine nature and the breadth of topics covered.
For the actual question; is this a normal thing in anti-magical religions? That is is it normal for such faiths to employ very obviously magical elements while simultaneously condemning nearly identical practices.

Pardon the horrifically rambling nature of this, and have a wonderful day.

JF

comments.title: Karmic Ties and Long Distance Travel
comments.content:
I'm in the process of emigrating. The reasons are complex, but let's just say I can see which way the winds are blowing and want to get out of my country before it becomes a full blown police state. This got me thinking about the karma of emigrating, which I've concluded is a mixed bag. However, one thought that did occur to me is that I'm likely leaving karmic ties behind, and will likely be making new ones in my new home. This got me thinking about casual long distance travel (and communication); and this led me to thinking it might have disastrous karmic consequences due to karmic ties.

Since most people generally remain close to where they were born, it seems likely to me that most karmic ties can be resolved by simply keeping souls in the same general areas. If the number of souls engaged in long distance travel is small, then they can probably be dealt with in relatively simple means. However, sometimes a large number of souls engage in long distance travel for a few centuries; such people would have ties in a wide range of areas. In order to resolve them, a lot of people would end up needing to live in a lot of different areas; and there would likely be reluctance on the part of at least some of them to return.

It thus no longer seems like a coincidence to me that periods of unusually high mobility tend to be followed by periods of unusually high forced mobility; the mass migrations of Early Dark Age Europe come to mind. If this analysis is right, then many, most, maybe even all of the souls being forced to flee their homes are being forced to a new area in order to resolve karmic ties. If I'm right, then casual long distance travel, especially the sort that sees people routinely live a few years in Europe, then a few years on the East Coast, then a couple years in Japan, and then a year or two in India, then the West Coast... before finally retiring to Hawaii is likely to rack up a lot of ties to a lot of places; which would mean either a very large number of lives, or at least one lifetime of being forced to travel to resolve these ties.

Making it even worse, a lot of people will take trips that, without fairly advanced technology, are fairly substantial, but seem pretty simple. Ottawa to Toronto, for instance, is about 200 miles; I made that trip at least once a month for several years. With a train, it's a few hours; but the trip used to take days or weeks. So it's likely, even if I remain here, I'd have karmic ties in at least two places which would be far enough apart it would either require two lifetimes to resolve, or require me to move between the two.

However, create enough ties in enough different places and the number of lifetimes, if only one set can be resolved, quickly increases beyond what is reasonable; especially with more complex ones that take more than one life anyway. As a result, with enough long distance travel, the set of karmic ties might be enough to start forcing people to move from place to place within a single lifetime.

I'm still considering the implications here, but does this preliminary analysis sound plausible to you?

replies.title: Re: Karmic Ties and Long Distance Travel
replies.content: Well, keep in mind that if two or more people have strong karmic ties connecting them, they're very likely to be reborn in the same place, or in places where they'll easily come into contact with one another. Also keep in mind that the universe has all the time it wants, and can easily let you work off this karmic burden in one life and that karmic burden in another. Myself, I suspect that a lot of what goes on in ages of high mobility amounts to working off karmic ties in a hurry, so that the next era can begin with a cleaner slate.

comments.content:
Wow. This meme is oddly poignant for me at the moment and for what I am about to write:

I have definitely noticed that I changed for the better in the last few years through my meditative practices and studies (including some of the guided studies on Ecosophia). I have shared with this community that I realized that I had been too involved in drinking during covid and I recently gave that up as it was masking a lot of emotional issues underneath. Since then , I have not only realized some of those emotional issues, but I also have some pretty profound shifts in my understanding of how the world works.

The reason I am writing today though is because I recently got hit in the face with my own character defect a few days ago that I have not yet worked through. The strangest thing is that i'm feeling a weird pole between having had these greater realizations and being smacked in the face by my own character defects. Magic and my own spiritual practices have brought me to great heights but I really notice that I need to change some of the more "ground-level" attributes that had me defaulting to some old thoughts/behaviors.

How do you - as practitioners of magic and as seekers of the truth - reconcile these two polarities of great realizations ans also being pulled humbly down by very mundane hanbits of the mind?

In fact, this old habit of thought came right on the heels of a very beautiful meditative session. Without getting into specifics, I realize that the over-arching demon of my life is shame, and I made the decision to over-extend my commitments to the point that my partner noticed it and even pointed it out. He keeps saying, "I don't know what debt you think you owe anyone".

For me it is kind of a jolt of humilitu in the sense that I have shared with him some pretty deep spiritual insights that I have had and yet I have not cleaned up my own house enough to the point that I was still totally controlled by these habits of thought. It's like a spiritual smack in the face. I think our host uses the metaphor of repeatedly running head first into a brick wall. *chuckles*.

Regardless, how do you all orient? Is this a sign that I let the transpersonal insights get me carried away? Or is this part of the whole thing: the uncovering of universal "outer" truths concurrent with my own "internal" universe's truths painful or joyful as those may be?

This is extremely common. It's routine in my life, for example, for some deep spiritual insight to be followed promptly by a realization that I need to fix some part of my personality that's still burdened by crap from childhood, or what have you. It's like repairing an old house -- it's when you tear out the plaster to put in something new and lovely that you discover that there are problems you didn't know about, and have to fix.

This is one of the reasons I recommend the Order of Spiritual Alchemy lessons, which are available for free download. Time spent digging through the unresolved experiences and traumas of childhood will not be wasted.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"How do you - as practitioners of magic and as seekers of the truth - reconcile these two polarities of great realizations ans also being pulled humbly down by very mundane hanbits of the mind?"

What you describe is familiar to me, too. One thing that has helped me is to do study programs that sit somewhere between psychology and religion / spirituality. Some occult and magic study programs will discuss the need to develop character and to wrestle with childhood issues, but don't offer much guidance about actually how to do so. The OSA is an exception.

I've found it necessary to read more recent books that have exercises in them, even if they belong to different traditions. There's sort of an as-yet-unnamed genre of this type of thing from the last few decades that draws on Jungian, Buddhist, and Christian traditions among others, and they focus more on emotional training rather than on thought training.

It's a bit of work to 'translate' between occult terminology and assumptions with those in these books, but in the end the techniques are, in my experience, effective regardless. A book such as Susan Thesenga's The Undefended Self (comes out of Eva Pierrakos' Pathwork) has a lot of overlap with the OSA; I've found both very helpful.

It does seem that you can have these realizations, the great heights you spoke of, and then get knocked down when facing some character defect. What's helped me is to feel fully whatever emotion, memory, or experience was involved, however unbearable, and more often than not, that has led to some pent-up energies being unlocked, leading back to the great heights and realizations!

The ups lead to the downs, but the downs lead back to the ups. Which is encouraging when stuck in the thick of it.

comments.title: Shuffling Method for the Sacred Geometry Oracle
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

while reading through the book that accompanies the Sacred Geometry Oracle I noticed that not only do the cards themselves have reservals built in, but also that in the provided sample readings these show up quite frequently.

So I was wondering what method of shuffling is appropriate for this as I am not familiar with using reversals.

Apparently one method is to cut the deck and flip one half before shuffling further. But that would „force“ reversals into the reading. Another one would be to spread the cards out on a surface and mix them with both hands, leaving the possibility for the cards to reverse. That necessitates quite a large surface however.

If you could shed some light on this, that would be much appreciated.

Thank you very much!

- Florian

replies.title: Re: Shuffling Method for the Sacred Geometry Oracle
replies.content:
Personally, I do a repeated overhand shuffle. Between each repetition, I cut and flip somewhere between the top quarter to the top three quarters of the deck.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Shuffling Method for the Sacred Geometry Oracle
replies.content: It's quite simple. Take the deck and cut it roughly in the middle, dividing it into two more or less evenly sized stacks. Put them face down on the table. Now rotate the two ends of the stacks that face away toward each other, and shuffle the two stacks together. Repeat this every time you shuffle, dividing the deck in half and rotating the halves in opposite directions, and you'll have half the cards reversed. Try it and see!

replies.title: Re: Shuffling Method for the Sacred Geometry Oracle
replies.content:
Thank you very much!

Just tried it out. That method really is simple.

- Florian

replies.title: Re: Shuffling Method for the Sacred Geometry Oracle
replies.content: It is, isn't it? I learned it from a tarot reader I knew more than forty years ago.

replies.title: Re: Shuffling Method for the Sacred Geometry Oracle
replies.content: Maybe I'm a bit unclear, myself.  Wouldn't reversing both piles 180 degrees put you right back where you started?  I reverse just one of the piles.  Maybe my brain is getting old.

replies.title: Re: Shuffling Method for the Sacred Geometry Oracle
replies.content: Imagine that you start with the top of the pile facing east. You divide it in half and then put the two piles side by side. Then you rotate one of them 90 degrees, so its top faces north, and the other 90 degrees the other way, so the top faces south. Then, without changing the orientation, you shuffle the two piles together. Does that make sense?

3) This sounds a little stupid but can you cleanse corners with the LBRP? My bed is in the corner of my bedroom, so if I feel the need to, I can do the SoP next to the bed and simply have the sphere expand until it encompasses the entire bed. If I were to do the LBRP, I obviously couldn't walk through the walls to the quarters.

4) I had an odd experience last week. Taking a walk, it seems that I held a short conversation with a god (Frey, to be specific). At some point, though, doubts started creeping in. Did a vastly greater being such as him really reach out to me, a puny mortal (and it was that way around; I wasn't praying or anything) or might this just be random mental chatter? While I won't ask you to speculate on a god's motivations¹, I'd like to know how one deals with this doubt. My guess would be to try to quiet the mind and see if I still get answers.

5) The above wasn't the odd part. The doubts intensified to a point of acute discomfort. Thoughts racing in circles, I very nearly convinced myself that I was just talking to myself and was going insane. Other, different doubts added themselves. At some point, with perhaps some nudging from Frey, I thought of doing the SoP but, as I wasn't the only one out for a walk in that particular forest, opted to wait for the privacy of my home.

This was a mistake. About five minutes to go, the ceaseless barrage of negative thoughts got bad enough that I started pleading for help. Immediately, I had a vision of myself with some sort of black worm-shaped thing woven through my mind. I was surrounded by several gods, who started pulling on that thing and smashing the bits they pulled out to a pulp². When done, some of it remained and they told me that I probably should still do the SoP when I get home. This was already a big relief and the SoP did the rest.

I'm at a complete loss as to what happened there. I'd be inclined to treat the black stuff very metaphorically, representing the doubts, but it's rather rare that those affect me so badly—I tend to need a few more drinks or at least a desolate walk home during the small hours to make me acutely think my grip on reality is slipping.

—David P.

1: Which, in retrospect, might simply have been amusement. I felt his presence a couple of times last week, each time accompanied with the same sense of amusement I get when I watch my cat do something silly.

That whole campaign probably deserves a post of its own one of these days, because it's such an epic display of how not to do collective magic. Some of the high points:

1) As long as the left remains stuck in the rut of resisting and opposing, without making some positive and appealing alternative the center of their campaign, they're going to keep on losing. comments.title: Saturn

replies.title: Re: Saturn
replies.content:
Look up planetary hours online or in any book on astrological magic, and find the planetary hours of Saturn on Saturday, which is of course Saturn's day. Every Saturday, during one of the hours of Saturn, light a dark blue candle and recite the Orphic Hymn to Saturn. You can also please Saturn by making donations to charities that help old people and the poor, both of which are under his rulership. The most important thing you must do, however, is to make Saturn's virtues of patience, prudence, discipline, and unyielding justice part of your life. I have a good relationship with Saturn -- it helps that he's well placed in my horoscope -- and have benefited immensely from that. Learn to work constructively with his energies and you will thrive.

comments.title: Update on an odd experience

Hi JMG and all,

Last week I reported on an odd experience I've had recently. A few weeks ago I was given an idea out of the blue to look up a person who had been important to me over 25 years ago.

The idea worked, and a day later I fell ill. In the fever-haze over the next two days, I confronted my relationship with this person and long-hidden feelings about them. It was painful, but facing those old negative feelings feels like a release.

I got interested in why this happened, so I cast a geomantic chart to inquire into why.

When I asked if this person was thinking of me, or trying to get in touch with me, I received this result:

RW: Caput Drac
LW: Cauda Drac
J: Carcer

I took that as a pretty forceful "No", with a sense of "what's done is done and you're stuck with it".

I followed up by asking why I'd had my recent visions of [person's name] out of the blue. This chart was more interesting:

RW: Rubeus
LW: Albus
J: Conjunctio

If I'm reading this well enough, it's saying that I'm bringing strong passion and emotional disturbance from my past, with a more peaceful calm and wise future ahead. Conjunctio suggests reconciling these opposing forces, coming to terms with what was "lost" and accepting the balance.

The house chart perfected with Fort. Maj. in occupation in the 1st and 9th houses, which says to me that the whole episode has been or will be to my benefit.

comments.title: generating reversed Tarot cards
comments.content:
(Deborah Bender here)

I have questions about reversed Tarot cards. Not how to read them; lots of books discuss that. My questions are how one generates reversals in the first place, starting from a deck that contains none, and whether a reader should allow reversed cards to accumulate or turn them upright after each reading.

The first book I read instructed me to fan the deck out face down, select three cards at random, reverse them and put them back in the deck in their original order before shuffling and cutting. This method would result in a gradual accumulation of reversed cards with the same cards coming up reversed time after time, until about half the deck is reversed, at which point reversals would become more random.

Another way would be to cut a new deck in half, turn one half around, and shuffle them like playing cards. This would make roughly half the cards reversed in any reading, but which cards they were would be more random.

I'm interested in what JMG and any other readers who modify the meaning of card when it is reversed think is the optimum proportion of reversed cards in the deck, and how they maintain that.

replies.title: Re: generating reversed Tarot cards
replies.content: I shuffle the cards in a way that guarantees roughly 50% reversals. The short form is that I cut the deck, then turn the top ends of the two halves toward each other (90 degree rotation in opposing directions -- it's a simple wrist move) and then shuffle them together. I do this every time I shuffle, so the deck becomes a thorough mix of upright and reversed. It seems to work.
